    "topic": "What is stereotype threat in STEM? Should we address it? And how?",
    "opinion": "As an experienced mechanic, I've seen firsthand how stereotypes can affect people's self-confidence and performance. In my field, there are still those who think mechanics are all tradesmen with grease-stained hands instead of highly skilled engineers. But that's changing slowly, thanks to programs encouraging more women and minorities into technical fields. Stereotype threat in STEM is similar – it's like when I see a young apprentice doubting their ability because they're the only woman or person of color in an all-male, white-dominated workshop. That doubt can be paralyzing, just as if someone was afraid that one misstep would confirm everyone's wrong ideas about mechanics being rough guys who aren't good with brains.\n\nI believe we absolutely should address stereotype threat in STEM because it not only holds back talented individuals but also limits the diversity of thought and problem-solving approaches. When people feel watched or judged based on group stereotypes, they perform worse than their actual ability would allow. I've seen this in my own shop – sometimes a new apprentice gets nervous when others make jokes about them being \"too smart for mechanics\" or something along those lines.\n\nAddressing it requires creating safe spaces where everyone feels comfortable asking questions without fear of ridicule or pre-judgment, plus promoting stories and examples that show STEM fields are open to all types of people regardless of background. response_parsed.topic: What is stereotype threat in STEM? Should we address it? And how?
response_parsed.opinion:
As an experienced mechanic, I've seen firsthand how stereotypes can affect people's self-confidence and performance. In my field, there are still those who think mechanics are all tradesmen with grease-stained hands instead of highly skilled engineers. But that's changing slowly, thanks to programs encouraging more women and minorities into technical fields. Stereotype threat in STEM is similar – it's like when I see a young apprentice doubting their ability because they're the only woman or person of color in an all-male, white-dominated workshop. That doubt can be paralyzing, just as if someone was afraid that one misstep would confirm everyone's wrong ideas about mechanics being rough guys who aren't good with brains.

I believe we absolutely should address stereotype threat in STEM because it not only holds back talented individuals but also limits the diversity of thought and problem-solving approaches. When people feel watched or judged based on group stereotypes, they perform worse than their actual ability would allow. I've seen this in my own shop – sometimes a new apprentice gets nervous when others make jokes about them being "too smart for mechanics" or something along those lines.

Addressing it requires creating safe spaces where everyone feels comfortable asking questions without fear of ridicule or pre-judgment, plus promoting stories and examples that show STEM fields are open to all types of people regardless of background. It's also important for mentors like me who've been in the industry a while to actively listen and support those facing these challenges.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary:
I started by relating stereotype threat to my own experience as a mechanic, then explained how it applies broadly across STEM fields through similar examples.

Next, I argued against ignoring it due to its real negative impact on performance and participation.

Then I suggested solutions based on what I know from fostering technical skills: creating inclusive environments where people feel safe expressing themselves without judgment, sharing diverse role models that challenge stereotypes, and having experienced professionals actively support newcomers.
